title: Chapter 12: The Golden Veins
content: The old library was three levels below the Keep, carved from salt and bone and forgotten decades. Dredd had never used it — the Rot King had preferred tribute to knowledge, blood to books — but the previous Viscount, the one Dredd had killed two centuries ago, had been a scholar. Her collection remained. Thousands of volumes on vampire law, Blood Pact theory, the Hierarchy, the Tribunal. A treasure trove that had sat untouched for two hundred years.

Sera was in the deepest chamber. The candlelight caught the golden veins in her arms, her neck, her face — they were brighter than Kael had ever seen them, tracing paths beneath her skin like rivers of liquid light. She was hunched over a massive tome bound in what looked like human leather, her fingers trembling as she turned the pages.
"You found something," Kael said.

"I found everything." Her voice was hoarse. She'd been reading for hours, he realized. Maybe all night. "The previous Viscount — her name was Elisabeta. She was a Blood Pact researcher. Spent three centuries studying the bond, documenting every variation, every anomaly. And she found something the Tribunal tried to bury."

She turned the tome toward him. The page was covered in cramped handwriting, diagrams of the Pact bond, annotations in a language Kael didn't recognize. But Sera had translated the key passage, her handwriting in the margin — neat, precise, the handwriting of a paladin who'd been trained to document everything.

*The anchor's transformation is not a disease. It is evolution. The Pact was never meant to be temporary. It was designed by the First Prince himself, millennia ago, as a mechanism for creating hybrid soldiers — beings who were neither human nor vampire, but something greater. Something immune to the weaknesses of both species. The Tribunal suppressed this knowledge because hybrid soldiers cannot be controlled by the Hierarchy. They are not bound by the invitation rule. They are not burned by sunlight. They are not subject to the Blood Curse.*
*The transformation completes when the vampire reaches Prince rank. Before that, the anchor is vulnerable. After that, the anchor is eternal.*
Kael read the passage twice. Three times. "Eternal."

"Not dying. Not transforming into something unrecognizable. *Eternal*. As long as you live, I live. As long as you're a Prince, I'm..." She trailed off, her hand going to her chest. The golden veins pulsed. "I'm whatever this is. Forever."
"That's good news."

"Is it?" She looked up at him, and he saw the fear Marcus had mentioned — a cold, quiet terror that had nothing to do with death and everything to do with the loss of self. "I've been preparing to die, Kael. Every day, watching the changes spread, I've been making peace with the idea that I'm not going to survive this. That I'm going to fade — become something else, something that isn't me. But this..." She tapped the tome. "This says I'm not dying. I'm *becoming*. And the thing I'm becoming isn't human and isn't vampire and isn't anything that's ever existed before."
"Is that so bad?"

"I don't know." Her voice cracked. "I don't know what I am anymore. I don't know if my father would recognize me. I don't know if *I* recognize me. The only thing I'm sure of is that I'm still your anchor. Still connected to you. Still..." She reached out and touched his face. Her fingers were warm — warmer than they'd been in weeks, as if the transformation was generating its own heat. "Still here."
Kael covered her hand with his. "Then that's enough. For now, that's enough."
"Is it?"
"It has to be. Because we've got bigger problems."
The bigger problem arrived three days later in the form of a letter.

The envelope was black silk, sealed with crimson wax. The handwriting inside was elegant and old — the handwriting of someone who'd been writing letters for centuries and had perfected the art of making words feel like weapons.
*To the self-proclaimed Viscount of Greyhaven:*

*Your ascension has not gone unnoticed. The manner of Lord Dredd's death raises certain questions. Questions about sunlight. About Blood Pacts. About a fledgling who rose too fast and learned too much.*

*I propose a meeting. Neutral ground. The Black Cathedral, three nights from now. Come alone, and we can discuss terms of mutual benefit. Refuse, and I will be forced to share my findings with the Tribunal. Specifically, with Prince Marius.*
*You have until moonrise to respond.*
*— Count Alessio Orsi, Lord of the Eastern Reach*
Kael read the letter aloud in the war room. Sera, Marcus, and Baron Kestrel — now his reluctant second-in-command — listened in silence.
"It's a trap," Marcus said.
"Obviously," Kael replied.
"He's going to try to kill you. Or blackmail you. Or—"

"He's going to try to blackmail me. The Veil Doctrine — he's threatening to expose what I am. Tell the Tribunal I've broken their oldest rules. Tell the human world vampires exist and one of them can walk in daylight." Kael set the letter down. "But he won't actually do it. The Veil protects him as much as it protects me. Without it, the human militaries mobilize. The Silver Dawn gets unlimited funding. It becomes a real war, and vampires lose."
"Then what does he want?"

"Leverage. He wants a weapon he can aim at his rivals. A daylight assassin who can eliminate his enemies while they sleep." Kael traced the map to the eastern territories. "Orsi has been consolidating power for centuries. He's got three Viscounts under him and he's aiming for Duke. If he can control me, he controls the only vampire in the world who can fight during the day."
"So what do we do?"
"We give him the meeting. What happens at the meeting..." Kael's smile was cold. "That depends on what I find."
That night, Sera came to his quarters. The golden veins had spread to her fingertips, and her eyes — once pale grey, now threaded with gold — glowed faintly in the dark.
"I want to go with you," she said. "To the Black Cathedral."
"Orsi said come alone."

"Orsi can go to hell." She crossed her arms. "I'm your anchor. If something goes wrong, if it's a trap, if he brings more Counts than he promised — I need to be there. The Pact works better at close range. You know that."
"I also know that if Orsi sees you, he'll know exactly what you are. An anchor. A weakness. A hostage."

"I'm not a weakness." The golden veins flared, and for a moment, the room was full of light. "I'm a paladin who's been absorbing Viscount-level power for a month. I'm faster than I've ever been. Stronger than I've ever been. And I'm not going to let you walk into enemy territory without me."
Kael studied her for a long moment. The candlelight caught the gold in her eyes and made them burn.
"Fine," he said. "But you stay hidden. If Orsi doesn't see you, he can't target you."
"And if he does see me?"
"Then we find out if Counts burn the same way Viscounts do."
